cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with reduced consciousness, shallow respirations, and pinpoint pupils.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.2; measured 26.8 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 61-year-old comes to ED worsening dyspnoea with productive cough and known COPD.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 66-year-old on haemodialysis presents with malaise and nausea after missing a recent dialysis session.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~32.0 (+/-2); measured 31.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (103 + 16) = 18, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 52-year-old is difficult to rouse with shallow respirations after multiple sedating medications.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.0; measured 27.5 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (28.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 76-year-old attends ED with chronic respiratory disease and increased drowsiness and productive cough and worsening dyspnoea.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 71-year-old taking loop diuretics presents with fatigue, dizziness, and clear contraction symptoms after ongoing fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.1; measured 50.3 is appropriate. Anion gap is 135 - (85 + 37) = 13, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~35.2; measured 34.7 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.4; measured 22.7 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.0; measured 45.4 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 26.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (117 + 12) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.1; measured 50.2 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.4; measured 33.2 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with heart failure reports cramps and light-headedness after escalating loop diuretics for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~45.6; measured 46.4 is appropriate. Anion gap is 138 - (96 + 32) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (20.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 54-year-old becomes sleepy with shallow breathing after a sedating treatment.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.4; measured 25.1 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 8.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 26-year-old presents with acute dyspnoea, dizziness, and hand paraesthesiae with a normal cardiorespiratory examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.4; measured 21.0 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old is brought to ED with pneumonia, tachypnoeic, and with rigors.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 33.1 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 140 - (113 + 18) = 9, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 42-year-old is drowsy with mildly reduced respiratory effort after taking sedating medication.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.1; measured 25.5 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 69-year-old taking loop diuretics reports postural dizziness and fatigue after several days of reduced intake.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.9; measured 44.8 is appropriate. Anion gap is 142 - (100 + 31) = 11, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 18-year-old comes to ED with general malaise and dehydration and nausea and abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 28.8 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (105 + 14) = 20 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.0; measured 22.5 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 24.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (116 + 12) = 9, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 61-year-old with renal impairment presents with weakness and poor intake after worsening uraemic symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~32.0 (+/-2); measured 30.9 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (104 + 16) = 19, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~33.5 (+/-2); measured 32.7 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (112 + 17) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.5; measured 28.4 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.
